solution_notes: Authoritative ground truth solutions. Each solution is comprehensive and covers all reasoning steps required by the rubric. This file is used as the reference answer when grading student submissions.

subparts.solution: False. Non-P is defined as the class of problems that can only be solved by non-polynomial algorithms, meaning no polynomial-time algorithm exists for them. NP is the class of problems verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine (equivalently, decidable in polynomial time by a nondeterministic Turing machine). These two classes are not necessarily disjoint. Whether they overlap depends on whether P equals NP. If P ≠ NP, then there exist problems in NP that are not in P — for instance, NP-complete problems such as 3SAT would be in NP but not in P, meaning they are also in Non-P. Therefore Non-P ∩ NP would be non-empty. The statement claims the intersection is always empty, which is false because it assumes that every NP problem is in P, which is precisely what the open P vs NP question asks.

True. The language {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0} is in P because it can be decided in linear time: scan the input, count the a's, count the b's, and check they are equal with no interleaving. Now suppose there exists a polynomial-time reduction from 3SAT to this language. Then to solve any instance of 3SAT, we apply the reduction in polynomial time to obtain an instance of {a^n b^n | n ≥ 0}, and then decide that instance in polynomial time. The composition of two polynomial-time procedures is polynomial, so 3SAT would be solvable in polynomial time, meaning 3SAT ∈ P. Since 3SAT is NP-complete, every language in NP can be polynomial-time reduced to 3SAT. Combined with 3SAT ∈ P, every NP language is also in P, so NP ⊆ P. Since P ⊆ NP always holds, we conclude P = NP.

False. P ≠ NP implies only that no polynomial-time algorithm exists for NP-complete problems such as 3-color. It says nothing whatsoever about a specific exponential lower bound. Even if we know 3-color has no polynomial algorithm, the best algorithm could still run in time 2^n, or O(1.89^n), or n^(log n) — all of which are super-polynomial but strictly less than 3^n. Establishing a lower bound of 3^n would require a completely separate complexity-theoretic argument. P ≠ NP is a statement about the non-existence of polynomial algorithms; it does not pin down where in the super-polynomial regime the best algorithm falls. Therefore the claim that any algorithm must take at least 3^n time does not follow from P ≠ NP alone.

True. 2SAT is known to be solvable in polynomial time (it can be solved in linear time using strongly connected components). If there exists a polynomial-time reduction from 3SAT to 2SAT, then any instance of 3SAT can be solved by first applying the reduction in polynomial time to produce a 2SAT instance, and then solving that 2SAT instance in polynomial time. The total time is polynomial, so 3SAT ∈ P. Since 3SAT is NP-complete, every language L in NP satisfies L ≤p 3SAT. With 3SAT ∈ P, it follows that every NP language is in P, so NP ⊆ P. Since P ⊆ NP always holds, P = NP. Therefore all NP-complete problems, and in fact all NP problems, can be solved in polynomial time.

False. The direction of this reduction is critical. A polynomial-time reduction from 2SAT to 3SAT (written 2SAT ≤p 3SAT) means that 2SAT is polynomial-time reducible to 3SAT, i.e., 2SAT is no harder than 3SAT. This is the easy direction — it says nothing useful because 2SAT is already in P and is trivially easier than 3SAT. To conclude that all NP problems are solvable in polynomial time, we would need the reverse: a reduction from an NP-complete problem to something already known to be in P. Specifically, we would need 3SAT ≤p 2SAT, meaning 3SAT can be reduced to 2SAT in polynomial time, which would place 3SAT in P. The given reduction 2SAT ≤p 3SAT does not imply this and gives no new information about solving 3SAT or any NP-complete problem efficiently.

False. A problem that is neither in P nor NP-complete does not have to be in NP at all. Consider the halting problem: it is undecidable, so it is not in P, and it is also not NP-complete (NP-complete problems are by definition in NP, and the halting problem is not even decidable). Yet the existence of the halting problem tells us nothing about whether P = NP. The statement P ≠ NP is a precise claim about the relationship between the complexity class P and the complexity class NP. It cannot be derived solely from the existence of a problem that happens to fall outside both P and NP-complete, since such a problem may lie entirely outside NP. Furthermore, even if the problem were in NP but not in P or NP-complete (an NP-intermediate problem, whose existence is guaranteed by Ladner's theorem assuming P ≠ NP), the implication would run the other way: P ≠ NP would imply NP-intermediate problems exist, not the converse.

questions.solution:
We prove that the Knapsack problem is in NP by constructing a polynomial-time verifier. Recall the decision version of Knapsack: given a set of n items where item i has weight w_i and value v_i, a weight capacity L, and a target value K, decide whether there exists a subset of items whose total weight is at most L and whose total value is at least K.

Certificate: A subset T ⊆ {1, ..., n} indicating which items are selected. This certificate has size O(n) and is therefore polynomial in the input size.

Verifier: On input (instance, T), the verifier computes two sums: the total weight Σ_{i ∈ T} w_i and the total value Σ_{i ∈ T} v_i. It then checks whether the total weight is at most L and whether the total value is at least K. If both conditions hold, it accepts; otherwise it rejects.

Polynomial time: Both summations require a single pass over T, taking O(n) time. The two comparisons take O(1). The total running time of the verifier is O(n), which is polynomial in the input size.

Completeness and soundness: If a valid subset T exists (i.e., the Knapsack instance is a yes-instance), then providing T as the certificate causes the verifier to accept. Conversely, if no valid subset exists, no certificate can cause the verifier to accept.

Since a polynomial-time verifier exists, the Knapsack problem is in NP.

We first state the decision version of the problem: given a weighted graph G = (V, E) with edge weights, two designated nodes s and t, and a number k, decide whether there exists a path from s to t whose total edge weight is at least k.

We prove this problem is in NP by constructing a polynomial-time verifier.

Certificate: A sequence of vertices (v_0, v_1, ..., v_m) representing a path from s to t. The certificate has length at most |V| and is therefore polynomial in the input size.

Verifier: On input (instance, certificate), the verifier performs the following checks:
  (1) Endpoint check: verify that v_0 = s and v_m = t.
  (2) Edge validity check: for each consecutive pair (v_i, v_{i+1}), verify that the edge (v_i, v_{i+1}) exists in E.
  (3) Weight check: compute the sum of weights of all edges (v_i, v_{i+1}) along the path and verify that the total is at least k.
If all three checks pass, the verifier accepts; otherwise it rejects.

Polynomial time: Check (1) takes O(1). Check (2) requires scanning m ≤ |V| edges, each verifiable in O(1) with an adjacency matrix or O(log |E|) with an adjacency list, giving O(|V|) total. Check (3) sums at most |V| weights in O(|V|) time. The total running time is O(|V| + |E|), which is polynomial in the input size.

Since a valid path of total weight at least k exists if and only if the verifier accepts on that path as certificate, the problem is in NP.

We describe a polynomial-time reduction from HAMILTONIAN to TONIAN, i.e., we show HAMILTONIAN ≤p TONIAN.

Construction: Given an instance G = (V, E) of HAMILTONIAN with |V| = n, construct a new graph G' = (V', E') as follows. Take two disjoint copies of G: let G1 = (V1, E1) and G2 = (V2, E2) where V1 and V2 are disjoint copies of V, and E1 and E2 are the corresponding copies of E. Set V' = V1 ∪ V2 and E' = E1 ∪ E2 with no edges between V1 and V2. Then |V'| = 2n.

Forward direction (G has a Hamiltonian path ⟹ G' has a Tonian path): Suppose G has a Hamiltonian path P that visits all n vertices of G exactly once. The copy of P within G1 is a path in G' that visits exactly n vertices. Since |V'| = 2n, this path visits n = |V'| / 2 vertices, which satisfies the Tonian condition of visiting at least half the vertices. Therefore G' has a Tonian path.

Backward direction (G' has a Tonian path ⟹ G has a Hamiltonian path): Suppose G' has a Tonian path P' visiting at least |V'| / 2 = n vertices. Since there are no edges between V1 and V2, any path in G' must lie entirely within G1 or entirely within G2. Each of G1 and G2 has exactly n vertices. Therefore P' visits at least n vertices within a single copy, say G1. But G1 has only n vertices in total, so P' visits all n vertices of G1 exactly once (since a path cannot revisit vertices). This corresponds to a Hamiltonian path in G.

Polynomial time: Constructing G' requires duplicating V and E, which takes O(|V| + |E|) time. This is polynomial in the size of G.

Conclusion: Since G has a Hamiltonian path if and only if G' has a Tonian path, and the construction runs in polynomial time, we have HAMILTONIAN ≤p TONIAN.

We prove MATCH is NP-complete by proving (1) MATCH ∈ NP and (2) MATCH is NP-hard.

--- Part 1: MATCH ∈ NP ---

Certificate: A binary string w ∈ {0,1}^n.

Verifier: On input (S, w), for every string s ∈ S, scan all n positions and check whether there exists at least one position i such that s_i = w_i. If this holds for every s ∈ S, accept; otherwise reject.

Polynomial time: For each of the |S| strings, we scan n positions. The total running time is O(|S| · n), which is polynomial in the input size |S| · n.

Therefore MATCH ∈ NP.

--- Part 2: MATCH is NP-hard (reduction from 3SAT) ---

We show 3SAT ≤p MATCH. Let φ be a 3SAT formula with n variables x_1, ..., x_n and m clauses C_1, ..., C_m.

Construction: For each clause C_j (1 ≤ j ≤ m), construct a string s^(j) ∈ {0, 1, 2}^n as follows:
  - s^(j)_i = 1  if x_i appears as a positive literal in C_j
  - s^(j)_i = 0  if x_i appears as a negative literal (¬x_i) in C_j
  - s^(j)_i = 2  if x_i does not appear in C_j
Let S = { s^(1), s^(2), ..., s^(m) }. This is the MATCH instance.

Forward direction (φ satisfiable ⟹ MATCH instance is True): Suppose φ has a satisfying assignment. Encode it as a binary string w ∈ {0,1}^n where w_i = 1 if x_i = True and w_i = 0 if x_i = False. Consider any clause C_j. Since the assignment satisfies φ, at least one literal in C_j is true. If that literal is x_i (positive), then w_i = 1 = s^(j)_i. If that literal is ¬x_i (negative), then w_i = 0 = s^(j)_i. In either case, w and s^(j) agree at position i. Since this holds for every clause C_j, w matches every string in S. Therefore the MATCH instance outputs True.

Backward direction (MATCH instance is True ⟹ φ satisfiable): Suppose there exists a binary string w ∈ {0,1}^n that matches every string in S. For each clause C_j, there is some position i where w_i = s^(j)_i. Since w_i ∈ {0, 1}, the matched position cannot have s^(j)_i = 2 (because 2 ∉ {0, 1}). Therefore s^(j)_i ∈ {0, 1}, meaning x_i appears in clause C_j. If w_i = s^(j)_i = 1, then x_i appears positively in C_j and w sets x_i = True, satisfying that literal. If w_i = s^(j)_i = 0, then ¬x_i appears in C_j and w sets x_i = False, satisfying that literal. In either case, clause C_j is satisfied. Since every clause is satisfied, the assignment induced by w satisfies φ.

Polynomial time of reduction: We construct m strings each of length n, requiring O(m · n) time, which is polynomial in the size of φ.

Conclusion: Since MATCH ∈ NP and 3SAT ≤p MATCH (so MATCH is NP-hard), MATCH is NP-complete.
